news_summary: Collaborative advancements in satellite technology and aerospace are enhancing our understanding of Earth's water resources and enabling breakthroughs in high-speed flight and stellar phenomena.
news_summary_fr: Les progrès collaboratifs dans les domaines de la technologie satellitaire et de l'aérospatiale améliorent notre compréhension des ressources en eau de la Terre et permettent des avancées décisives dans le domaine des vols à grande vitesse et des phénomènes stellaires.
news_summary_es: Los avances colaborativos en tecnología satelital y aeroespacial están mejorando nuestra comprensión de los recursos hídricos de la Tierra y permitiendo avances revolucionarios en vuelos de alta velocidad y fenómenos estelares.

articles.title: US-French Satellite Takes Stock of World’s River Water
articles.summary: In a first, a space mission led by NASA and France has tracked Earth’s rivers swelling and shrinking from month to month over the course of a year and found significantly less of a swing than previous model-based estimates. A record drought in the Amazon likely influenced the tally made by the Surface Water and […]
articles.content:
Sunlight glints off one of the solar panels of the SWOT satellite in this artist’s concept. The antennas of the mission’s key instrument — the Ka-band Radar Interferometer (KaRIn) — collect data along a swath 30 miles (50 kilometers) wide on either side of the satellite. CNES

In a first, a space mission led by NASA and France has tracked Earth’s rivers swelling and shrinking from month to month over the course of a year and found significantly less of a swing than previous model-based estimates. A record drought in the Amazon likely influenced the tally made by the Surface Water and Ocean Topography (SWOT) satellite. The findings also reveal new details about the underwater topography of the world’s river channels.

Launched in 2022, SWOT is a collaboration between NASA and the French space agency CNES (Centre National d’Études Spatiales). It is the first satellite capable of surveying not only the ocean, but also nearly all the world’s lakes and rivers with ultraprecision. While SWOT does not measure the absolute volume of rivers, it can track their width, surface height, and slope changing over time.

Traditionally, hydrologists have relied on models to calculate river storage changes, or they multiplied altimeter estimates of height by optical or radar estimates of width. In contrast, SWOT measures both dimensions, height and width, at the same time using its sensitive Ka-band Radar Interferometer (KaRIn) instrument to bounce microwaves off the water’s surface and time how long the signal takes to return. The new study, published Wednesday in Nature, analyzed nearly 1.6 million such observations.

The analysis paints a picture of some 127,000 river segments rising and falling between October 2023 and September 2024. In aggregate, river volumes varied by almost 83 trillion gallons (313 cubic kilometers). That’s about 28% less of a swing than the lowest previous estimates, a result likely skewed by extremely dry conditions during that period in the Amazon, home to Earth’s largest river by volume.

Earth’s rivers pulse like capilleries in this visualization using data from the SWOT mission. The world tour zooms in on iconic rivers including the Amazon, which in the span of a year gained and lost enough water to fill 68 million Olympic-size swimming pools.

NASA’s Scientific Visualization Studio

New way to map river channels

Even gripped by drought, the Amazon River varied more than any other during the year, gaining and losing more than 45 trillion gallons (172 cubic kilometers) — enough to cover the entire state of California in more than a foot of water.

More surprisingly, the world’s longest river, the Nile, varied less than expected, with volumes changing by only 2.2 trillion gallons (8.5 cubic kilometers). Possible explanations include upstream damming and severe drought, along with challenges that come with learning to work with a new satellite instrument.

Cedric David, who leads the SWOT research team that conducted the work at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory in Southern California, said the findings are a first look and the role of large floodplain dynamics remain to be fully determined. Still, such an accounting has been elusive until now. River gauges are sparse in areas, and some channels too remote for boat and ground surveys. Longstanding questions, such as how fast do rivers flow and how much rainwater and snowmelt runs into them, have added to the uncertainty.

“We’re starting to untangle some of the really tough questions SWOT was built for,” David said. “This is just the beginning.”

Tracking rivers as they swell and shrink is also helping scientists visualize something that can be challenging to survey in person: the underlying shape of riverbanks and beds. Such contours influence everything from shipping to flooding but have remained largely unmapped in many places, noted Arnaud Cerbelaud, a postdoctoral research fellow at JPL who co-led the study.

The new SWOT data provides a window into river channels ranging from concave to convex, steep to gentle, and stable to highly variable. In the Amazon, Mississippi, Orinoco, Yangtze, Ganges, Mekong and Yenisei rivers, for example, observed water levels vary by more than 32 feet (10 meters) from peak to trough.

“The implications go far beyond hydrology and will help us understand how water moves through the global Earth system,” Cerbelaud said.

More about SWOT

Launched in December 2022 from Vandenberg Space Force Base in California, SWOT is now in its operations phase, collecting data that will be used for research and other purposes.

The SWOT satellite was jointly developed by NASA and CNES, with contributions from the CSA (Canadian Space Agency), and the UK Space Agency. NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory, managed for the agency by Caltech in Pasadena, California, leads the U.S. component of the project. For the flight system payload, NASA provided the KaRIn instrument, a GPS science receiver, a laser retroreflector, a two-beam microwave radiometer, and NASA instrument operations. NASA partners at CNES provided the Doppler Orbitography and Radioposition Integrated by Satellite system, the dual frequency Poseidon altimeter (developed by Thales Alenia Space), the KaRIn radio-frequency subsystem (together with Thales Alenia Space and with support from the UK Space Agency), the satellite platform, and ground operations. The KaRIn high-power transmitter assembly was provided by CSA. NASA provided the launch vehicle and the agency’s Launch Services Program, based at Kennedy Space Center in Florida, managed the associated launch services.

To learn more about SWOT, visit:

https://swot.jpl.nasa.gov

News Media Contacts

Andrew Wang / Andrew Good

Jet Propulsion Laboratory, Pasadena, Calif.

626-379-6874 / 818-393-2433

andrew.wang@jpl.nasa.gov / andrew.c.good@jpl.nasa.gov

Written by Sally Younger

2026-014
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a significant scientific achievement by NASA and French space agency CNES using the SWOT satellite to precisely track global river water changes and underwater topography. This breakthrough provides valuable data with broad implications for hydrology, environmental monitoring, and understanding Earth's water systems, benefiting society and science worldwide.
articles.personality_title: New satellite reveals how Earth’s rivers change throughout the year
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA and the French space agency CNES launched a satellite called SWOT in 2022 to study Earth’s water. This satellite can measure the size and height of almost all the world’s lakes and rivers with great accuracy.

**What happened** – Over one year, SWOT tracked how rivers around the world grew and shrank each month. Scientists found that rivers changed less than previous estimates suggested. For example, the Amazon River, even during a drought, changed more than any other river, while the Nile changed less than expected. SWOT uses a special radar instrument called KaRIn to measure river width and height at the same time.

**Impact** – This is the first time scientists have had such detailed and accurate information about river changes worldwide. It helps answer questions about how much water rivers hold and how they flow, especially in places where there were few or no measurements before. The data also shows the shapes of riverbeds and banks, which affect flooding and shipping.

**What's next step** – Scientists will continue to study the data from SWOT to better understand how rivers work and how water moves on Earth. This will improve water management and environmental protection in the future.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The SWOT satellite is giving scientists a clearer picture of how rivers change over time, improving our knowledge of Earth’s water systems for the first time.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouveau satellite révèle comment les rivières de la Terre changent toute l’année
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La NASA et l’agence spatiale française CNES ont lancé en 2022 un satellite appelé SWOT pour étudier l’eau sur Terre. Ce satellite peut mesurer avec grande précision la taille et la hauteur de presque tous les lacs et rivières du monde.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Pendant un an, SWOT a suivi la croissance et la diminution mensuelles des rivières dans le monde. Les scientifiques ont découvert que les changements étaient moins importants que ce que les modèles précédents avaient estimé. Par exemple, le fleuve Amazone, même en période de sécheresse, a changé plus que tout autre fleuve, tandis que le Nil a changé moins que prévu. SWOT utilise un radar spécial appelé KaRIn pour mesurer la largeur et la hauteur des rivières en même temps.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois que les scientifiques disposent d’informations aussi détaillées et précises sur les variations des rivières à l’échelle mondiale. Cela aide à répondre aux questions sur la quantité d’eau que les rivières contiennent et leur débit, surtout dans des endroits où il y avait peu ou pas de mesures auparavant. Les données montrent aussi la forme des lits et berges des rivières, ce qui influence les inondations et la navigation.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques continueront d’étudier les données de SWOT pour mieux comprendre le fonctionnement des rivières et le mouvement de l’eau sur Terre. Cela améliorera la gestion de l’eau et la protection de l’environnement à l’avenir.

**Phrase clé** – Le satellite SWOT offre aux scientifiques une image plus claire des changements des rivières dans le temps, améliorant pour la première fois notre connaissance des systèmes d’eau de la Terre.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo satélite revela cómo cambian los ríos de la Tierra durante el año
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La NASA y la agencia espacial francesa CNES lanzaron en 2022 un satélite llamado SWOT para estudiar el agua en la Tierra. Este satélite puede medir con gran precisión el tamaño y la altura de casi todos los lagos y ríos del mundo.

**Qué pasó** – Durante un año, SWOT siguió cómo los ríos crecían y disminuían cada mes. Los científicos descubrieron que los cambios eran menores de lo que los modelos anteriores habían estimado. Por ejemplo, el río Amazonas, incluso durante una sequía, cambió más que cualquier otro río, mientras que el Nilo cambió menos de lo esperado. SWOT usa un radar especial llamado KaRIn para medir a la vez el ancho y la altura de los ríos.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que los científicos tienen información tan detallada y precisa sobre los cambios de los ríos en todo el mundo. Esto ayuda a responder preguntas sobre cuánta agua contienen los ríos y cómo fluyen, especialmente en lugares donde antes había pocas o ninguna medición. Los datos también muestran la forma del lecho y las orillas de los ríos, lo que afecta las inundaciones y el transporte.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos seguirán estudiando los datos de SWOT para entender mejor cómo funcionan los ríos y cómo se mueve el agua en la Tierra. Esto mejorará la gestión del agua y la protección ambiental en el futuro.

**Resumen en una frase** – El satélite SWOT está dando a los científicos una imagen más clara de cómo cambian los ríos con el tiempo, mejorando por primera vez nuestro conocimiento de los sistemas de agua de la Tierra.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a sleek satellite with solar panels and radar antennas orbiting above a glowing, gently flowing network of major world rivers—like the Amazon and Nile—depicted as vibrant, shimmering blue ribbons winding across a softly colored Earth, highlighting subtle variations in river widths and water levels with delicate light reflections on their surfaces.

articles.title: High-Speed Flight Project Overview
articles.summary: What We do The High-Speed Flight (HSF) project develops technologies that make high-speed, airbreathing, commercial flight possible from Mach 1 to Mach 5 and above. HSF creates tools, technologies, and knowledge that will help eliminate today’s technical barriers to practical supersonic flight, most notably sonic boom. The project supports the X-59 quiet supersonic vehicle testing […]
articles.content:
Artist concept of a high-speed point-to-point vehicle. NASA Langley

What We do

The High-Speed Flight (HSF) project develops technologies that make high-speed, airbreathing, commercial flight possible from Mach 1 to Mach 5 and above.

HSF creates tools, technologies, and knowledge that will help eliminate today’s technical barriers to practical supersonic flight, most notably sonic boom. The project supports the X-59 quiet supersonic vehicle testing by gathering acoustic data and validating tools that predict in-flight sonic booms.

HSF conducts fundamental and applied research that explores key challenges in reusable, hypersonic flight technology.

Future Applications

The project evaluates the potential for future commercial hypersonic vehicles, including reusable access to space and commercial point-to-point missions.

Unique Hypersonic Facilities and Expertise

NASA maintains unique facilities, laboratories, and subject matter experts who investigate fundamental and applied research areas to solve the challenges of hypersonic flight. The High-Speed Flight project coordinates closely with partners in industry, academia, and other government agencies to leverage relevant data sets to validate computational models. These partners also utilize NASA expertise, facilities, and computational tools. Partnerships are critical to advancing the state of the art in hypersonic flight.

Contact the High-Speed Flight Project by email at larc-htp-inquiries@mail.nasa.gov
articles.reasoning: The article describes NASA's High-Speed Flight project, which is developing technologies to enable practical supersonic and hypersonic commercial flight, including overcoming sonic boom challenges. This represents a significant technological breakthrough with broad potential impact on future air travel and space access, supported by substantial research, partnerships, and unique facilities.
articles.personality_title: NASA advances technology for faster, quieter supersonic flights
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA is working on the High-Speed Flight (HSF) project to create technology that allows airplanes to fly much faster than usual—between Mach 1 (the speed of sound) and Mach 5 and above. Right now, flying this fast causes loud sonic booms, which are a big problem.

**What happened** – The HSF project is developing tools and knowledge to fix these problems, especially by reducing the noise of sonic booms. The project supports testing of the X-59, a special quiet supersonic plane, by collecting sound data and checking how well their predictions about sonic booms work. NASA also studies how to make reusable hypersonic planes that could fly very fast and be used many times.

**Impact** – This work is important because it could change how people travel by air, making flights much faster without disturbing people on the ground with loud noises. It could also help with future space travel by making it easier and cheaper to reach space. NASA’s unique labs and experts, along with partnerships with universities and companies, make this research possible.

**What's next step** – NASA will continue testing and improving the technology, especially with the X-59 plane, to better understand and control sonic booms. They will also explore new types of hypersonic vehicles that can be reused and used for quick trips around the world or to space.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s High-Speed Flight project is building the technology needed for faster, quieter supersonic and hypersonic flights that could transform air and space travel.
articles.personality_title_fr: La NASA développe des technologies pour des vols supersoniques plus rapides et plus silencieux
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La NASA travaille sur le projet High-Speed Flight (HSF) pour créer des technologies permettant aux avions de voler beaucoup plus vite que d’habitude — entre Mach 1 (la vitesse du son) et Mach 5 et plus. Actuellement, voler aussi vite provoque des bangs soniques forts, ce qui pose un problème important.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le projet HSF développe des outils et des connaissances pour résoudre ces problèmes, notamment en réduisant le bruit des bangs soniques. Le projet soutient les essais de l’avion supersonique silencieux X-59 en recueillant des données acoustiques et en vérifiant la précision des prévisions sur les bangs soniques. La NASA étudie aussi comment fabriquer des avions hypersoniques réutilisables, capables de voler très vite et d’être utilisés plusieurs fois.

**Impact** – Ce travail est important car il pourrait changer la façon dont les gens voyagent en avion, en rendant les vols beaucoup plus rapides sans déranger les personnes au sol avec des bruits forts. Cela pourrait aussi faciliter les voyages dans l’espace en les rendant plus faciles et moins coûteux. Les laboratoires uniques et les experts de la NASA, ainsi que les partenariats avec des universités et des entreprises, rendent cette recherche possible.

**Prochaine étape** – La NASA continuera à tester et améliorer la technologie, surtout avec l’avion X-59, pour mieux comprendre et contrôler les bangs soniques. Elle explorera aussi de nouveaux types de véhicules hypersoniques réutilisables pour des trajets rapides autour du monde ou vers l’espace.

**Une phrase clé** – Le projet High-Speed Flight de la NASA construit la technologie nécessaire pour des vols supersoniques et hypersoniques plus rapides et plus silencieux qui pourraient transformer les voyages aériens et spatiaux.
articles.personality_title_es: La NASA avanza en tecnología para vuelos supersónicos más rápidos y silenciosos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La NASA está trabajando en el proyecto High-Speed Flight (HSF) para crear tecnología que permita a los aviones volar mucho más rápido de lo normal — entre Mach 1 (la velocidad del sonido) y Mach 5 o más. Actualmente, volar tan rápido causa fuertes estampidos sónicos, lo que es un problema.

**Qué pasó** – El proyecto HSF desarrolla herramientas y conocimientos para solucionar estos problemas, especialmente reduciendo el ruido de los estampidos sónicos. El proyecto apoya las pruebas del avión supersónico silencioso X-59, recogiendo datos acústicos y verificando qué tan bien predicen los estampidos sónicos. NASA también investiga cómo hacer aviones hipersónicos reutilizables que puedan volar muy rápido y usarse varias veces.

**Impacto** – Este trabajo es importante porque podría cambiar cómo viajan las personas en avión, haciendo los vuelos mucho más rápidos sin molestar a la gente en tierra con ruidos fuertes. También podría ayudar en futuros viajes al espacio, haciéndolos más fáciles y baratos. Los laboratorios y expertos únicos de NASA, junto con socios en universidades y empresas, hacen posible esta investigación.

**Próximos pasos** – NASA continuará probando y mejorando la tecnología, especialmente con el avión X-59, para entender y controlar mejor los estampidos sónicos. También explorarán nuevos tipos de vehículos hipersónicos reutilizables para viajes rápidos alrededor del mundo o al espacio.

**Una frase clave** – El proyecto High-Speed Flight de NASA está desarrollando la tecnología necesaria para vuelos supersónicos e hipersónicos más rápidos y silenciosos que podrían transformar los viajes aéreos y espaciales.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a sleek, futuristic supersonic aircraft soaring above softly glowing clouds with gentle sonic wave ripples radiating outward, set against a warm sky of muted blues and soft oranges, symbolizing advanced high-speed flight and the quiet breakthrough of sonic boom technology.

articles.title: Blowing Stellar Bubbles
articles.summary: For the first time, a young, Sun-like star has been caught red-handed blowing bubbles in the galaxy, by astronomers using NASA’s Chandra X-ray Observatory. The bubble – called an “astrosphere” – completely surrounds the juvenile star in this image released on Feb. 23, 2026. Winds from the star’s surface are blowing up the bubble and […]
articles.content:
X-ray: NASA/CXC/John Hopkins Univ./C.M. Lisse et al.; Infrared: NASA/ESA/STIS; Image Processing: NASA/CXC/SAO/N. Wolk

For the first time, a young, Sun-like star has been caught red-handed blowing bubbles in the galaxy, by astronomers using NASA’s Chandra X-ray Observatory.

The bubble – called an “astrosphere” – completely surrounds the juvenile star in this image released on Feb. 23, 2026. Winds from the star’s surface are blowing up the bubble and filling it with hot gas as it expands into much cooler galactic gas and dust surrounding the star. The Sun has a similar bubble around it, which scientists call the heliosphere, created by the solar wind. It extends far beyond the planets in our solar system and protects Earth from cosmic radiation.

This is the first image of an astrosphere astronomers have obtained around a star similar to the Sun. It shows slightly extended emission, rather than a single point of light as seen for other such stars.

Read more about this discovery.

Text credit: Lee Mohon

Image credit: X-ray: NASA/CXC/John Hopkins Univ./C.M. Lisse et al.; Infrared: NASA/ESA/STIS; Image Processing: NASA/CXC/SAO/N. Wolk
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery: the first direct observation of an astrosphere around a young, Sun-like star. This finding enhances our understanding of stellar phenomena similar to our Sun's protective heliosphere, which has broad implications for astrophysics and space science. The discovery is well-detailed and represents a meaningful advancement with potential benefits for the scientific community and public knowledge.
articles.personality_title: Astronomers capture first image of bubble around young star like our Sun
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Stars like our Sun send out winds of charged particles that create protective bubbles around them. Our Sun’s bubble is called the heliosphere and helps shield Earth from harmful space radiation. Until now, scientists had never seen such a bubble, called an astrosphere, clearly around a young star similar to the Sun.

**What happened** – Using NASA’s Chandra X-ray Observatory, astronomers took the first clear image of an astrosphere surrounding a young, Sun-like star. This image, released on February 23, 2026, shows the star blowing a bubble filled with hot gas that pushes out into the cooler gas and dust nearby. The bubble looks larger and more spread out than the star’s usual point of light.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it helps scientists understand how stars like our Sun protect themselves and their planets from space radiation. Seeing an astrosphere around a young star gives clues about how such bubbles form and evolve over time. It also improves our knowledge of space environments that might affect planets around other stars.

**What's next step** – Researchers will study more young stars to see if they also have astrospheres like this one. This will help build a bigger picture of how common these protective bubbles are and how they change as stars grow older. Future observations might also explore how these bubbles influence the habitability of planets.

**One-sentence takeaway** – For the first time, scientists have directly seen a protective bubble called an astrosphere around a young star like the Sun, opening new insights into how stars and their planets stay safe from cosmic radiation.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les astronomes capturent la première image d'une bulle autour d'une jeune étoile comme le Soleil
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les étoiles comme notre Soleil envoient des vents de particules chargées qui créent autour d’elles des bulles protectrices. La bulle du Soleil s’appelle l’héliosphère et protège la Terre des radiations spatiales nocives. Jusqu’à présent, les scientifiques n’avaient jamais clairement vu une telle bulle, appelée astrosphère, autour d’une jeune étoile similaire au Soleil.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Grâce à l’observatoire à rayons X Chandra de la NASA, des astronomes ont pris la première image nette d’une astrosphère entourant une jeune étoile ressemblant au Soleil. Cette image, publiée le 23 février 2026, montre l’étoile soufflant une bulle remplie de gaz chaud qui repousse le gaz et la poussière plus froids autour d’elle. La bulle apparaît plus grande et étendue que la lumière habituelle de l’étoile.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle aide les scientifiques à comprendre comment les étoiles comme le Soleil se protègent, ainsi que leurs planètes, des radiations spatiales. Voir une astrosphère autour d’une jeune étoile donne des indices sur la formation et l’évolution de ces bulles. Cela améliore aussi notre connaissance des environnements spatiaux qui peuvent influencer les planètes autour d’autres étoiles.

**Prochaines étapes** – Les chercheurs étudieront d’autres jeunes étoiles pour voir si elles possèdent aussi des astrosphères. Cela permettra de mieux comprendre la fréquence de ces bulles protectrices et leur évolution avec l’âge des étoiles. Les futures observations pourraient aussi explorer comment ces bulles affectent la possibilité de vie sur les planètes.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Pour la première fois, des scientifiques ont vu directement une bulle protectrice appelée astrosphère autour d’une jeune étoile comme le Soleil, ouvrant de nouvelles pistes sur la façon dont les étoiles et leurs planètes se protègent des radiations cosmiques.
articles.personality_title_es: Astrónomos capturan la primera imagen de una burbuja alrededor de una estrella joven como el Sol
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las estrellas como nuestro Sol envían vientos de partículas cargadas que crean burbujas protectoras a su alrededor. La burbuja del Sol se llama heliosfera y ayuda a proteger la Tierra de la radiación espacial dañina. Hasta ahora, los científicos nunca habían visto claramente una burbuja así, llamada astrosfera, alrededor de una estrella joven similar al Sol.

**Qué pasó** – Usando el Observatorio de Rayos X Chandra de la NASA, los astrónomos tomaron la primera imagen clara de una astrosfera que rodea a una estrella joven parecida al Sol. Esta imagen, publicada el 23 de febrero de 2026, muestra a la estrella creando una burbuja llena de gas caliente que se expande hacia el gas y polvo más fríos a su alrededor. La burbuja se ve más grande y extendida que la luz normal de la estrella.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque ayuda a los científicos a entender cómo las estrellas como el Sol se protegen a sí mismas y a sus planetas de la radiación del espacio. Ver una astrosfera alrededor de una estrella joven da pistas sobre cómo se forman y cambian estas burbujas con el tiempo. También mejora nuestro conocimiento sobre los ambientes espaciales que podrían afectar a los planetas en otras estrellas.

**Próximos pasos** – Los investigadores estudiarán más estrellas jóvenes para ver si también tienen astrosferas como esta. Esto ayudará a crear una imagen más completa de cuán comunes son estas burbujas protectoras y cómo cambian a medida que las estrellas envejecen. Observaciones futuras podrían explorar cómo estas burbujas influyen en la posibilidad de que los planetas sean habitables.

**Resumen en una frase** – Por primera vez, los científicos han visto directamente una burbuja protectora llamada astrosfera alrededor de una estrella joven como el Sol, abriendo nuevas ideas sobre cómo las estrellas y sus planetas se protegen de la radiación cósmica.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing young star at the center, surrounded by a translucent, expanding bubble of swirling hot gas that gently pushes outward into a cooler, textured cosmic background of dust and galactic material, rendered in soft, natural hues of amber, deep blue, and muted gray, symbolizing the star’s protective astrosphere in a serene and dynamic cosmic environment.
